narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and a family from a high-income bracket, you've shown real strengths in your background that many students aspire to. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45.5%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize applicants with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in law school placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar. Counterbalancing that significantly was your undergraduate GPA, which had a strong positive pull toward predicting bar passage, as it exceeds most applicants' records and suggests you have the foundational academic capability. Your LSAT score of 32, though lower than the average 36.7, also nudged the prediction slightly toward success but carried less weight than your other grades. Being a full-time student, like over 90% of applicants, didn't sway things much either way. In essence, while your undergrad record shines brighter than most, the model weighed your weaker Year 3 decile most heavily, viewing it as the biggest red flag for bar outcomes relative to typical students who rank higher in later years. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a supportive family background—many in similar circumstances overcome early hurdles—but the system's focus on these patterns led to the no. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews that might value your full profile. You could also seek bar prep resources early to build a track record of improvement, turning this into a stepping stone for even stronger future applications. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
